finding: An age-related decline in episodic detail retrieval for past events also manifests for future-event imagination. Relational memory ability correlated with episodic detail generation in older adults.
Prototypical-view targets increased first-fixation likelihood; action-defined targets reduced fixation duration. Both effects were stronger in the lower visual field.
Exposure to deterministic messaging increased unethical behavior, mediated by reduced free-will belief. Free-will endorsement prevented cheating in a separate task.
During attentional blink, temporal selection is suppressed, delayed, and diffused in time, with each effect showing distinct time-course recovery patterns.
Extremal edges on convex surfaces served as a strong depth cue, causing regions bearing an extremal edge to be perceived as closer than non-extremal regions.
Auditory change detection performance decreased with stimulus complexity uniformly across interstimulus intervals, unlike visual change detection which shows interval-dependent complexity effects.
Object words denoting spatially-biased entities (head/foot) oriented attention toward those entities' typical visual locations, interfering with target identification.
High external motivation to avoid prejudice produced initial attentional approach toward neutral Black faces but later avoidance; happy expressions eliminated these biases.
Error commission primed defensive motivation; the error-related negativity magnitude predicted the strength of startle potentiation following errors.
Loneliness (chronic or induced) increased both anthropomorphism of nonhuman agents and belief in anthropomorphized religious agents; negative affect alone did not produce this effect.
Adjustment away from a numerical anchor was smaller when the anchor was precise than when rounded, reflecting finer subjective-scale resolution for precise anchors.
Partnered women preferred attached men at low fertility and single masculine men at high fertility; attachment status effects reversed with conception risk.

Amygdala activation flexibly tracked evaluative goals (positive, negative, or combined); valence-driven activation occurred only when aligned with task goals, with weaker modulation for positive information.
Cognitive disfluency increased abstract (global) over concrete (local) construal; disfluency was interpreted as psychological distance, promoting higher-level construal.
Survival-relevance rating produced superior memory retention compared to imagery, pleasantness, self-reference, generation, intentional learning, and contextually-rich scenario rating.
Preschoolers used speaker gaze to infer referential intent only when consistent with linguistic and pragmatic context; they did not blindly follow gaze.

Phonological activation during language production was modulated by language-specific word order; English (noun-first) showed object-naming effects, French (adjective-first) showed color-naming effects.
Implicit attitudes generalized to new group members immediately via associative links; explicit attitudes resisted generalization initially but converged with implicit attitudes after forgetting.
Glucose ingestion reduced reliance on heuristic-based choice, reversing the attraction effect that arose after mental resource depletion.
Nonconscious goal priming facilitated implicit learning of novel environment structure, supporting productive (not just reproductive) nonconscious goal pursuit.
